title: Adaptive Reuse Is Eating New Construction
verdict: The character premium on converted buildings now exceeds the efficiency premium on new ones in select submarkets.
evidence: Across a sample of 40 adaptive reuse projects completed between 2022 and 2025, converted industrial, institutional, and commercial buildings achieved rental premiums of 8% to 15% over comparable new construction in the same submarkets. The effect was strongest in markets with established creative economies, including Brooklyn, downtown Los Angeles, and Philadelphia's post-industrial corridors. Tenants consistently cited character, ceiling height, and architectural distinctiveness as primary drivers of their leasing decisions, ranking these above HVAC efficiency and modern floor plate dimensions. Construction cost data further favors conversion: adaptive reuse projects came in at roughly 60% to 75% of ground-up costs per square foot in the same markets.
frame: This is not a trend driven by nostalgia. It is a rational market response to the fact that character is scarce and new construction is abundant. In submarkets where both options exist, tenants and residents are voting with their wallets for the converted warehouse over the glass-and-panel new build. For developers and investors, the math is compelling on both sides of the ledger: lower basis going in, higher rents coming out. The risk is in execution; adaptive reuse demands a design team that can read an existing structure's potential, not just a pro forma jockey running yield scenarios. The buildings that get it right become neighborhood landmarks. The ones that get it wrong become expensive renovation stories.
